title: Minimax and alpha-beta

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Minimax and alpha-beta; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Fundamental Issues and Basic Search Strategies: state spaces, uninformed/informed, and adversarial search; atomic topic: Minimax and alpha-beta
sources.claim: Minimax backs terminal or cutoff utility through alternating maximizing and minimizing choices, while alpha-beta skips branches that cannot change the backed-up decision under the same ordering and evaluation assumptions.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Minimax backs terminal or cutoff utility through alternating maximizing and minimizing choices, while alpha-beta skips branches that cannot change the backed-up decision under the same ordering and evaluation assumptions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A depth-two zero-sum tree alternates max and min layers, backs leaf values, and selects the root action with the highest worst-case result. Your goal is to trace and implement bounded minimax with alpha-beta pruning for a small game. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Agent, goal and utility models; dsa graphs bfs dfs; States, actions, goals and costs; dsa recursion design. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Minimax backs terminal or cutoff utility through alternating maximizing and minimizing choices, while alpha-beta skips branches that cannot change the backed-up decision under the same ordering and evaluation assumptions. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Depth cutoffs require a heuristic evaluation, real opponents need not minimize the modeled utility, chance nodes require another model, move order affects pruning not minimax value, and utility design embeds preferences. The learner must reject this overclaim: Alpha-beta changes the minimax answer to a faster approximation and can prune any branch whose first leaf looks poor.

includes: Back up utility through alternating choices and prune branches that cannot affect the decision.
Trace and implement bounded minimax with alpha-beta pruning for a small game.
excludes: Depth cutoffs require a heuristic evaluation, real opponents need not minimize the modeled utility, chance nodes require another model, move order affects pruning not minimax value, and utility design embeds preferences.

outcomes: Trace and implement bounded minimax with alpha-beta pruning for a small game.

examples.title: Minimax and alpha-beta: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A depth-two zero-sum tree alternates max and min layers, backs leaf values, and selects the root action with the highest worst-case result.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A depth-two zero-sum tree alternates max and min layers, backs leaf values, and selects the root action with the highest worst-case result.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Alpha-beta preserves the minimax value when bounds and alternating roles are correct; prune only after the branch cannot affect an ancestor choice.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Alpha-beta preserves the minimax value when bounds and alternating roles are correct; prune only after the branch cannot affect an ancestor choice.

examples.title: Minimax and alpha-beta: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Alpha-beta visits a strong move first and prunes a sibling branch only after its bound cannot improve the ancestor decision.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Alpha-beta visits a strong move first and prunes a sibling branch only after its bound cannot improve the ancestor decision.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Depth cutoffs require a heuristic evaluation, real opponents need not minimize the modeled utility, chance nodes require another model, move order affects pruning not minimax value, and utility design embeds preferences.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: Alpha-beta changes the minimax answer to a faster approximation and can prune any branch whose first leaf looks poor.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace and implement bounded minimax with alpha-beta pruning for a small game.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Alpha-beta changes the minimax answer to a faster approximation and can prune any branch whose first leaf looks poor.
misconceptions.correction: Alpha-beta preserves the minimax value when bounds and alternating roles are correct; prune only after the branch cannot affect an ancestor choice.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: Alpha-beta changes the minimax answer to a faster approximation and can prune any branch whose first leaf looks poor.

analogy.example: a route planner that tracks states, legal moves, accumulated cost, estimates, and an ordered frontier can introduce Minimax and alpha-beta only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: state-space representation, frontier policies, heuristics, and adversarial backup
limitations: real environments can be continuous, stochastic, changing, partially observed, or too large for the simplified route model
